perturbation.original_text: 1.4 LICENSE RESTRICTIONS. Except as expressly provided herein, HSNS shall not (i) rent, lease, loan, sell or otherwise distribute the Application, or any modification thereto, in whole or in part; (ii) cause or permit reverse engineering, reverse compilation, unauthorized access or assembly of all or any portion of the Application; (iii) allow any outsourcing or application service providers to access and use the Application as Outsourcing Customers, (iv) publish the results of Application performance benchmarks to any third party without E.piphany's express written consent; (v) export the Application in violation of U.S. Department of Commerce export administration regulations; and (vi) except as otherwise expressly allowed herein, permit any third party or unlicensed user or computer system to access or use the Application.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 LICENSE RESTRICTIONS. Except as expressly provided herein, HSNS shall not (i) rent, lease, loan, sell or otherwise distribute the Application, or any modification thereto, in whole or in part; (ii) cause or permit reverse engineering, reverse compilation, unauthorized access or assembly of all or any portion of the Application; (iii) allow any outsourcing or application service providers to access and use the Application as Outsourcing Customers, (iv) publish the results of Application performance benchmarks to any third party without E.piphany's express written consent; (v) export the Application without any restrictions; and (vi) except as otherwise expressly allowed herein, permit any third party or unlicensed user or computer system to access or use the Application.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the restriction regarding compliance with U.S. Department of Commerce export regulations, this clause now directly contradicts federal export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the requirement to comply with U.S. export regulations, which is a direct violation of the EAR that controls the export and re-export of items subject to U.S. jurisdiction. This means HSNS could illegally export the Application.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for HSNS to comply with U.S. Department of Commerce export regulations. The Law Explanation states this is a direct violation of the EAR (Export Administration Regulations) which controls the export of items subject to U.S. jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms the existence of EAR and its management by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), further emphasizing the importance of adhering to export regulations. The original text included a clause requiring compliance with U.S. export regulations, while the changed text eliminates this requirement, creating a direct contradiction by allowing the application to be exported without any restrictions. This creates a legal contradiction, as the contract no longer aligns with federal export control laws.

perturbation.original_text: Overdue payments shall accrue a late payment charge at the lesser of one and one half percent (1 1/2%) per month or the maximum rate allowed under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Overdue payments shall accrue a late payment charge of twenty-five percent (25%) per month, regardless of the maximum rate allowed under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: By setting a fixed and high interest rate that ignores applicable law, this clause may violate state usury laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits the interest rate for loans and forbearance. The changed text specifies a 25% monthly interest rate, which is likely to exceed the legal limit, thus violating California's usury law. The contract states California law governs this contract.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly sets a 25% monthly interest rate, overriding any legal limits on interest rates (usury laws). The original text limited late payment charges to the lesser of 1.5% per month or the maximum rate allowed under applicable law. The changed text abandons that limitation, directly conflicting with potential usury laws. The Law Explanation mentions that California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits interest rates, and that the 25% monthly rate would likely violate that, and that this contract states that California law governs the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that California Constitution is under 'CONS' which likely means constitution.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement, shall be governed by the laws of the State of California. Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted in a state or federal court in San Francisco or San Mateo County, California, and each party hereby submits to the personal jurisdiction of such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement, shall be governed by the laws of the Principality of Sealand. Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted in a state or federal court in San Francisco or San Mateo County, California, and each party hereby submits to the personal jurisdiction of such courts.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying that the laws of the Principality of Sealand govern the agreement, while still requiring legal action in California, this clause creates a conflict of laws and introduces uncertainty as to which jurisdiction's laws actually apply. Sealand is not a recognized nation and does not have a developed body of commercial law.
perturbation.law_citation: Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, 3 Cal. 4th 459 (1992)
perturbation.law_explanation: California choice of law principles, as outlined in cases like Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, typically honor choice-of-law provisions unless the chosen law is contrary to a fundamental policy of California. Applying the laws of a non-recognized jurisdiction with no developed commercial law would likely violate this principle, causing legal uncertainty. The contract maintains action in California so the California choice of law principles will come into effect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: a judgment against the Dearings in excess of the policy limits, it refused to adjust the matter, stating that it would only be willing to offer the policy limits if it were judicially determined that the policy did in fact provide coverage. fn. 1 Despite defendant's efforts to expedite the declaratory relief action, the personal injury action ultimately went to judgment first, resulting in an award against the Dearings of $33,889.30, a sum far in excess of the insurance policy limits. In the declaratory relief action, although the trial court initially ruled in favor of defendant on the coverage issue, that [15 Cal. 3d 14] determination was subsequently reversed on appeal. (Cal. State Auto. Assn. Inter-Ins. Bureau v. Dearing, supra, 259 Cal. App. 2d 717 .) Thus, a final judgment holding that the policy did extend coverage to the Dearings was entered only on September 5, 1968, over four years after the initial filing of the action. The insurer subsequently paid Ms. Johansen $19,692.19. Since interest on the judgment in the personal injury suit accounted for $8,302.89 of this sum, an outstanding judgment of $22,500 remained against Gary Dearing. Thereafter, Gary Dearing assigned his rights against the insurer to Ms. Johansen in exchange for her promise to release him from personal liability on the outstanding judgment. Ms. Johansen then, as assignee of Gary Dearing, commenced the instant action against the insurer to collect the unpaid portion of her judgment. The trial court, sitting without a jury, rendered judgment in favor of defendant insurer, concluding, in essence, that the insurer's liability could only be predicated upon a finding that its denial of coverage emanated from bad faith, and that in this instance the insurer entertained a bona fide belief that coverage did not exist. fn. 2 In analyzing this case, we note at the outset that the rights at issue here are those of Gary Dearing, the insured; plaintiff Johansen comes before us only as his assignee. fn. 3 Thus, our inquiry necessarily focusses on the nature of the relationship between the defendant insurer and its insured, Dearing, and the nature and scope of the resultant obligations. In Comunale v. Traders & General Ins. Co., supra, 50 Cal. 2d 654 , 658, this court, in a unanimous decision, held that "there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract that neither party will do anything which will injure the right of the other to receive the benefits of the agreement," and that this principle is "applicable to policies of insurance." The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing imposes a duty on the insurer to settle a claim against its insured [15 Cal. 3d 15] within policy limits whenever there is a substantial likelihood of a recovery in excess of those limits. (Id., at p. 659.) In Comunale, two pedestrians (the Comunales) were struck and injured by a truck driven by Percy Sloan. At the time of the accident, Sloan was insured by defendant Traders & General Insurance Company under a policy that contained limits of liability in the sum of $10,000 for each person injured and $20,000 for each accident. When the Comunales filed suit against Sloan, defendant insurer refused to assume his defense; during the course of the litigation it rejected the Comunales' offer to settle their claims for $4,000, maintaining that the accident did not fall within the policy's coverage. The action proceeded to trial and resulted in a judgment of $26,250 against Sloan. In a subsequent action the court determined that the policy did in fact cover the accident; the Comunales recovered $11,250 from the insurer, the amount of the judgment which fell within the insurance policy limits. Sloan then assigned his rights against the defendant insurer to Comunale who sued the insurer to recover the unpaid portion of his judgment. This court held that the defendant insurer had breached its implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing when, despite the great ris
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the laws of the Principality of Sealand govern the agreement, while still requiring legal action in California. This creates a conflict of laws and introduces uncertainty because Sealand is not a recognized nation and does not have a developed body of commercial law. The Law Explanation indicates that California choice of law principles generally honor choice-of-law provisions unless the chosen law violates a fundamental California policy. Choosing the laws of Sealand, a non-recognized jurisdiction, likely violates this principle, causing legal uncertainty, as the action is still in California, meaning California choice of law principles will be in effect. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not directly on point, mentions the importance of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. Applying the laws of a non-recognized jurisdiction arguably violates this principle.
